sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] not in ['w', 'g']:
        print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After UP:
- The blue-gray rectangle was at (28,28)-(31,39) - 4x12. Now it's at (28,28)-(31,35) - 4x8. So it shrank by 4 columns on the right!
- The white strip is now at (28,36)-(32,39) - 5x4, positioned to the RIGHT of the blue-gray rectangle.
- The player is at (33,36)-(35,39) - 3x4.
So UP on the blue-gray rectangle caused it to shrink from 4x12 to 4x8, and the white strip moved to the right side of it. This is different from what happened with the charcoal!
With the charcoal, UP converted it to G. With the blue-gray, UP shrunk it.
Let me continue using UP to shrink the blue-gray further. Maybe the goal is to shrink it completely or to a certain size.
Let me try UP again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue-gray shrank from 4x12 to 4x8 after UP. White strip is now at right side.
# Try UP again to shrink it further.
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
